evidence: Verified verbatim against the primary source. PMC12232517 is McCarthy S, Motala A, Lawson E, Shekelle PG, "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV," BMJ Quality & Safety, 2025. WebFetch confirmed the exact sentence: "Two systematic reviews of Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation (SBAR) (including 11 and 28 original research studies; 5 and 15 were about the use in handoffs) and two newer original research studies provided low certainty evidence that the SBAR tool improves patient safety outcomes." Every element of the claim maps 1:1 to the quote (low certainty rating; two prior SRs; 11 and 28 studies; 5 and 15 handoff-specific; two newer original studies). Independent corroboration: the AHRQ/NCBI Bookshelf version of the same MHS IV report (NBK613742; effectivehealthcare.ahrq.gov structured-handoff rapid review, Jan 2025) states "two systematic reviews and two new original research studies (one a randomized controlled trial) provided low-certainty evidence that use of the SBAR tool can improve patient safety clinical outcomes," and contrasts it with I-PASS (moderate certainty, "stronger than the evidence supporting SBAR"), consistent with the claim's "only LOW" framing. The two cited SBAR systematic reviews are Müller et al. 2018 and Lo et al. 2021. The only near-qualifier found — Müller 2018 self-described its findings as "moderate evidence" — does not contradict the claim, which correctly attributes the LOW certainty rating to the 2025 MHS IV review's own assessment. Source is a current (2025), peer-reviewed, government-commissioned systematic review; quality matches claim strength; not marketing; not outdated.

Verified directly against PMC12232517 (McCarthy, Motala, Lawson, Shekelle, "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV," BMJ Quality & Safety, 2025). Every element of the claim matches the source: (1) "We found 16 articles (2 systematic reviews and 13 primary studies published in 14 publications)" — the claim reproduces the source's own enumeration; (2) "We judged the certainty of evidence that the use of I-PASS reduces medical errors and adverse events as moderate, uprated from low (based on study design)"; (3) the abstract conclusion is a verbatim match: "The SBAR and I-PASS structured tools for within-unit handoffs probably improve patient safety, with I-PASS having a stronger certainty of evidence"; (4) "strongest of any tool reviewed" is supported by "Of the plethora of structured handoff tools proposed, I-PASS is the tool that has the strongest certainty of evidence in reducing medical errors" (SBAR was rated low certainty; other tools like iHAND/ISBAR3 had less). Independently corroborated by the AHRQ Effective Health Care rapid-review PDF and NCBI Bookshelf NBK613742 ("Ten original research studies... provided moderate certainty evidence that the I-PASS tool reduces medical errors and adverse events"). Source is an AHRQ-commissioned peer-reviewed systematic review (2025) — quality matches claim strength; not outdated; not marketing. Caveat for downstream use (not a refutation): the review states I-PASS evidence is exclusively from physician handoffs in academic settings and SBAR evidence is predominantly nursing — "Whether or not one generalises to the other is not established" — and the moderate rating was uprated from low. The claim accurately reports the review's conclusions without overreach.

Direct fetch of PMC12232517 ("Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV," BMJ Quality & Safety 2025, PROSPERO CRD42024576324, GRADE-modified certainty assessment) confirms the supporting quote verbatim: "Two systematic reviews of Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation (SBAR) (including 11 and 28 original research studies; 5 and 15 were about the use in handoffs) and two newer original research studies provided low certainty evidence that the SBAR tool improves patient safety outcomes." Every element of the claim (LOW certainty rating, two prior systematic reviews with 11 and 28 studies, 5 and 15 handoff-specific, plus two newer original studies) matches one-to-one with no overreach. The article further states "We agreed with the systematic review on SBAR that the certainty/quality of evidence for the effect of SBAR on those same outcomes is low," confirming this is the review's own rating, and contrasts it with moderate-certainty evidence for I-PASS (10 studies) in the same article, consistent with the "same review" attribution. Independent confirmation via NCBI Bookshelf NBK613742 and AHRQ EHC versions of the MHS IV report. Source is a high-quality peer-reviewed AHRQ systematic review published April 2025 — current and well-matched to the claim's strength. Caveat for the parent synthesis (not a refutation): the review notes nearly all included studies assessed physician-to-physician handoffs in academic teaching hospitals, limiting direct generalization to nursing shift report.

Verified directly against the primary source (McCarthy S, Motala A, Lawson E, Shekelle PG. "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV." BMJ Qual Saf 2025, PMC12232517) and cross-checked against the AHRQ MHS IV rapid review version (NCBI Bookshelf NBK613742) and independent search snippets. All claim elements confirmed verbatim: (1) Abstract Results: "We found 16 articles (2 systematic reviews and 13 primary studies published in 14 publications) that met our eligibility criteria" — the claim's count matches the source's own framing (13 studies in 14 publications + 2 SRs = 16 articles); (2) "moderate certainty evidence that the I-PASS tool ... reduces medical errors and adverse events" is verbatim; (3) the quoted conclusion "The SBAR and I-PASS structured tools for within-unit handoffs probably improve patient safety, with I-PASS having a stronger certainty of evidence" is verbatim, followed by "Other published tools lack sufficient evidence to draw conclusions"; (4) "strongest evidence rating of any tool reviewed" holds — SBAR received low certainty, iHAND/SIGNOUT had a single ungradeable study, and no tool exceeded I-PASS's moderate rating. No erratum, retraction, or credible contradicting source found. Source quality (peer-reviewed BMJ Qual Saf systematic review from AHRQ's MHS IV program, April 2025) matches claim strength and is current. Caveat for downstream use (does not refute the claim): the review itself notes the I-PASS evidence is largely from the original developer group, in academic teaching settings, and concerns physician handoffs — so applying it to nursing shift report requires the nursing-adaptation literature.

Quote verified verbatim via WebFetch of https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12232517/ — the article (McCarthy S, Motala A, Lawson E, Shekelle PG, "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV," BMJ Quality & Safety 2025;34(10):680, an AHRQ-commissioned peer-reviewed systematic review published Jan 2025) contains the exact sentence: "Two systematic reviews of Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation (SBAR) (including 11 and 28 original research studies; 5 and 15 were about the use in handoffs) and two newer original research studies provided low certainty evidence that the SBAR tool improves patient safety outcomes." The claim restates this faithfully with no overreach: LOW certainty rating, two prior SRs with 11 and 28 original studies (5 and 15 handoff-specific, respectively), plus two newer original studies. WebFetch confirmed the article assigns low certainty to SBAR consistently throughout and never a moderate/high rating (I-PASS, by contrast, gets moderate certainty). The two underlying SRs are Müller et al. 2018 and Lo et al. 2021; Müller 2018 (BMJ Open, PMID 30139905) comprised 8 before-after studies + 3 controlled clinical trials = 11 studies, independently corroborating the "11" figure. Source is current (Jan 2025, within the 2024–2026 window), authoritative (AHRQ MHS IV / BMJ Qual Saf), and not marketing. No credible source disputes that this review assigned low certainty.

Claim verified against the cited source plus two independent corroborating versions. (1) PMC12232517 (McCarthy, Motala, Lawson, Shekelle, "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV," BMJ Quality & Safety 2025;34(10):680) states verbatim: "We found 16 articles (2 systematic reviews and 13 primary studies published in 14 publications)" — the claim's counts match the source's own phrasing (13 primary studies appeared in 14 publications, hence 16 articles total). (2) The results state "moderate certainty evidence that the I-PASS tool reduces medical errors and adverse events" (10 studies of 9 implementations, 2 RCTs) — exactly the supporting quote. (3) SBAR was rated LOW certainty ("low-certainty evidence that use of the SBAR tool can improve patient safety clinical outcomes") and "no multisite evidence was found for any other structured handoff tool" (only a single iHAND/SIGNOUT study), so moderate for I-PASS is genuinely the strongest rating of any tool in the review. (4) The conclusion quote is verbatim: "The SBAR and I-PASS structured tools for within-unit handoffs probably improve patient safety, with I-PASS having a stronger certainty of evidence." Corroborated independently by NCBI Bookshelf NBK613742 and the AHRQ Effective Health Care report PDF. No contradicting source found; source is an AHRQ-commissioned peer-reviewed systematic review (2024-2025), current as of June 2026. CAVEAT for downstream use (does not refute the claim as written): the review itself notes the I-PASS clinical-outcomes evidence is exclusively from PHYSICIAN handoffs, while SBAR evidence is predominantly from nurse-involved handoffs — important for the nursing-handoff research question; also MHS IV rapid-review methods were abridged, which authors note may limit certainty/generalizability.

Verified against the full text of the AHRQ MHS IV review (NCBI Bookshelf NBK613742, published January 2025; direct PDF returned empty on fetch but the Bookshelf and PMC versions are the same review). The quoted sentence appears verbatim: "Ten studies (of 9 implementations; 2 studies were randomized controlled trials) provided moderate-certainty evidence that the I-PASS tool...can improve patient safety clinical outcomes." All claim specifics match: 10 studies, 9 implementations, 2 RCTs, moderate certainty, patient safety clinical outcomes. The "strongest evidence rating" assertion goes beyond the quote but is independently confirmed: SBAR received only low-certainty evidence, no other tool received a certainty rating, and the review's conclusion explicitly states I-PASS has "a stronger certainty of evidence" than SBAR. Scope check: the review covers intrahospital within-unit transitions (shift changes); the peer-reviewed journal version is itself titled "within-hospital unit transitions" (PMC12232517, 2025), so the claim's phrasing tracks the authors' own. Source is a current (Jan 2025) government evidence review, also peer-reviewed published — quality matches claim strength; not a press release (the I-PASS Institute press release exists separately but is not the cited source). Non-fatal caveats the parent should carry forward: nearly all included studies were physician-to-physician handoffs in academic teaching hospitals, and the review notes I-PASS benefits were less convincingly demonstrated in implementations outside the original development team — the claim does not assert nursing-specific outcomes, so these qualify rather than refute.

Verified against the primary source (AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV, published January 2025; full text at https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK613742/ and peer-reviewed in J Hosp Med, PMC12232517). The report states verbatim that "10 studies about 9 implementations of I-PASS or modifications" (two of them RCTs) provided "moderate-certainty evidence that the I-PASS tool...can improve patient safety clinical outcomes" — exactly matching the claim's numbers. The comparative assertion ("strongest evidence rating in the review") is also confirmed: the report rates SBAR/ISBAR evidence as low certainty, explicitly says I-PASS has "a stronger certainty of evidence" than SBAR, and found "no multisite evidence" for any of the ~12 other tools evaluated — so moderate (I-PASS) is the highest rating given. Source quality matches claim strength (federal evidence review + peer-reviewed publication), it is current (Jan 2025), and no contradicting source was found in two searches. Two minor, non-fatal nuances: (1) the report's precise scope is "intrahospital WITHIN-UNIT transitions" (the claim says "within-hospital transitions"; the authors' own journal title uses "within-hospital unit transitions," so the paraphrase is faithful but slightly broad); (2) the moderate rating was uprated from low via GRADE domains (theory/logic models, analogous safety interventions), and the report cautions that data come mainly from academic teaching hospitals with strongest effects from the original I-PASS development team — qualifications, not contradictions, since the claim accurately reports what the review concluded and preserves the "moderate-certainty" hedge.

Verified against the primary source and its peer-reviewed twin. (1) The quoted sentence appears verbatim in the AHRQ MHS IV rapid review (Shekelle, Motala, Lawson; published January 2025; NCBI Bookshelf NBK613742 / AHRQ PDF): "Ten studies (of 9 implementations; 2 studies were randomized controlled trials) provided moderate-certainty evidence that the I-PASS tool...can improve patient safety clinical outcomes." (2) The "strongest rating in the review" clause checks out: SBAR received only low-certainty evidence (from 2 systematic reviews + 2 newer studies), no other handoff tool received a certainty rating (single-implementation evidence deemed insufficient), and the peer-reviewed version (BMJ Quality & Safety, 2025; PMC12232517) explicitly describes I-PASS as the tool with "the strongest certainty of evidence in reducing medical errors." (3) Source quality is top-tier for the claim: a federal AHRQ evidence review plus peer-reviewed journal publication, not the I-PASS Institute press release. (4) Current: January 2025 publication, no superseding or contradicting source found in searches as of June 2026. Two faithful-use caveats, neither rising to refutation: the review's scope is precisely "intrahospital WITHIN-UNIT transitions" (shift-to-shift in the same unit, excluding between-unit transfers) — the claim's "within-hospital transitions" is marginally broader, though the BMJ title itself reads "within-hospital unit transitions"; and the underlying studies are predominantly academic-hospital, physician-to-physician handoffs, a generalizability limitation the review states for nursing applications.

Quote verified verbatim via WebFetch of PMC12232517: the article is "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV" (BMJ Quality & Safety, 2025, AHRQ-commissioned systematic review — high-quality source matched to claim strength). It states exactly: "Evidence about SBAR use comes predominantly from handoffs where nurses are involved (either nurse-to-nurse or nurse-to-physician)" and "The evidence supporting the effect of I-PASS use on clinical outcomes is exclusively about physician handoffs and comes almost entirely from the developers." The claim's "superior evidence rating" premise is also grounded in the same source: the review judged I-PASS evidence as moderate certainty for reducing medical errors/adverse events vs low certainty for SBAR. Adversarial contradiction search found NO nursing-handoff I-PASS study with clinical outcomes: the landmark Starmer 2023 multicenter study (32 hospitals, 47% adverse-event reduction; PubMed 36326255) enrolled 2,735 resident physicians + 760 faculty, not nurses; the "I-PASS nursing handoff bundle" study (PSNet) measured only communication quality and workflow; the newest nursing study (Abt et al., J Adv Nursing 2026 pilot, PubMed 40084437) measured handover global quality, patient trust, feasibility, and acceptability — no adverse events/medical errors. Only vendor marketing (I-PASS Institute case studies) claims nursing clinical-outcome wins, which the review's developer-COI caveat anticipates. The inference (I-PASS clinical-outcome superiority not demonstrated for nursing shift report) follows logically from the verified quotes and is the review's own applicability caveat, not an overreach. Claim is current (review published 2025; checked against 2026 literature).

Quote verified verbatim in PMC12232517 = AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV systematic review, BMJ Quality & Safety 2025 (PRISMA, GRADE, PubMed/EMBASE/PSNet/CINAHL, 2013-2024). Source states: "Evidence about SBAR use comes predominantly from handoffs where nurses are involved (either nurse-to-nurse or nurse-to-physician)" and "The evidence supporting the effect of I-PASS use on clinical outcomes is exclusively about physician handoffs and comes almost entirely from the developers." The claim's 'superior evidence rating' framing is also grounded in the same source: I-PASS certainty rated MODERATE for reducing medical errors/adverse events vs LOW for SBAR ("I-PASS is the tool that has the strongest certainty of evidence in reducing medical errors"), so the inference that this superiority is undemonstrated for nursing shift report follows directly. Active refutation attempts all failed: (1) the flagship nursing I-PASS study (Starmer 2017, PMID 28679836, PICU pre-post) measured only communication quality and workflow, explicitly NOT medical errors/adverse events ("may therefore have the potential to... reduce medical errors"); (2) an April 2026 scoping review of I-PASS in nurse handovers (journalmpci.com, 9 studies including 2025 nursing papers post-dating the review's search window) found no nursing study measuring clinical outcomes — only communication quality, satisfaction, time efficiency, engagement; (3) the major I-PASS clinical-outcome trials (Starmer NEJM 2014, 23% error reduction; J Hosp Med 2023 multicenter 'diverse clinical environments' study, DOI 10.1002/jhm.12979, 47% harm reduction) enrolled 2,735 resident physicians + 760 faculty — physician handoffs by the developer group. Source quality (AHRQ-commissioned systematic review) matches claim strength; claim is current (2025 review, corroborated by 2026 literature); not marketing — it pushes back against developer-driven I-PASS evidence.

Quote verified verbatim in the source, which is high-quality and current: 'Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV' (AHRQ-commissioned), BMJ Quality & Safety, 2025 (PMC12232517). The review states 'Evidence about SBAR use comes predominantly from handoffs where nurses are involved (either nurse-to-nurse or nurse-to-physician)' and 'The evidence supporting the effect of I-PASS use on clinical outcomes is exclusively about physician handoffs and comes almost entirely from the developers.' The claim's 'superior evidence rating' framing matches the review's own ratings (I-PASS moderate certainty vs SBAR low certainty; 'I-PASS is the tool that has the strongest certainty of evidence in reducing medical errors'), and the inference that this has not been demonstrated for nursing shift report follows directly from 'exclusively physician handoffs.' Adversarial search found NO counterexample: the I-PASS clinical-outcome trials are all physician populations (Starmer NEJM 2014, resident physicians; Starmer J Hosp Med 2023, 32 hospitals, 2,735 resident physicians, 47% adverse-event reduction; Argentina PICU stepped-wedge, residents), while nursing I-PASS studies measure only process/perception outcomes, not clinical outcomes (Starmer 2017 BMJ Qual Saf I-PASS Nursing Handoff Bundle, PMID 28679836 — communication quality, interruptions, workflow only, no errors/adverse events measured; Blazin 2020 Pediatr Qual Saf, PMID 32766496 — clinician self-reported perceptions only). No 2024-2026 nursing I-PASS clinical-outcome study surfaced that would make the claim outdated. Not marketing: the independent federal review explicitly discounts developer-driven evidence. Minor caveat only: the 2023 multicenter study may have involved some APPs at some sites, but APP signout is not nursing shift report, so the caveat for a nursing handoff platform stands.

Claim verified against the primary source. PubMed record (PMID 40084437) confirms the article "I-PASS-Structured Bedside Nursing Handovers: A Type-1 Effectiveness-Implementation Hybrid Pilot Study" (Abt M, Schneider P, Martin L, Delmas P, Ortoleva Bucher C), Journal of Advanced Nursing 2026;82:882-896, published online 14 March 2025, DOI 10.1111/jan.16896. Every claim element checks out: (1) "Swiss pilot" - all authors at La Source School of Nursing, HES-SO University of Applied Sciences and Arts Western Switzerland, Lausanne; PubMed setting = Switzerland; "Pilot Study" is in the title; (2) "two wards (one surgery, one medicine)" - abstract: "Bedside nursing handovers were introduced into one surgery and one medicine ward"; (3) "I-PASS-structured" - confirmed by the title; nursing adaptation expanded as Identification-Patient-Action-Situation-Synthesis; (4) "831 evaluated handovers" - abstract: "the 831 handovers evaluated"; (5) "significant improvements in handover quality compared to before implementation" - verbatim abstract text (design: simple interrupted time series, 3 measurement points before/after, Aug-Nov 2022); (6) "handover duration increased" - verbatim abstract text. The supporting quote is authentic abstract text, not fabricated. No contradicting evidence found in searches; a separate scoping review of I-PASS nurse handover quality is directionally consistent. Source is current (online 2025, issue Jan 2026) peer-reviewed primary research, not marketing. The claim faithfully includes the unfavorable duration finding, arguing against cherry-picking. Minor caveat only: abstract says "significant" without printing p-values (full text paywalled, HTTP 402); reading this as "statistically significant" is the conventional interpretation of a quantitative ITS results statement and not an overreach.

Quote verified verbatim in the peer-reviewed version of the cited AHRQ review (Making Healthcare Safer IV rapid review, published as 'Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review' in BMJ Quality & Safety 2025, PMC12232517; search window Jan 2013-June/July 2024). Discussion section states: 'Evidence about SBAR is predominantly nursing, and evidence about I-PASS is exclusively about physicians. Whether or not one generalises to the other is not established.' The claim's components each check out: (1) the review judged I-PASS evidence moderate certainty for reducing medical errors/adverse events, based on 10 studies that were physician-to-physician (or physician/NP-PA provider) handoffs in academic teaching hospitals; (2) 'Nurses were the clinicians in all included studies' of SBAR; (3) the inference that moderate-certainty I-PASS outcome data has not been demonstrated in nurse-to-nurse shift report is explicitly licensed by the review's own generalization caveat. Adversarial search found nursing I-PASS adaptation studies (e.g., I-PASS Nursing Handoff Bundle, PMID 28679836; I-PASS BATON quasi-experimental nursing study), but these measured communication quality, workflow, or safety culture rather than patient-safety clinical outcomes, so they do not contradict the claim as scoped to the review's graded effectiveness evidence. One minor looseness: 'exclusively about physicians' is the review authors' own summary even though some I-PASS studies included NP/PA providers - still not nurse-to-nurse shift report, so the substantive point stands. Source is a current (2025) government evidence review plus peer-reviewed publication - quality matches claim strength; not outdated, not marketing.

Quote verified verbatim in the peer-reviewed version of the cited AHRQ review (McCarthy, Motala, Lawson, Shekelle, "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions," Making Healthcare Safer IV; BMJ Quality & Safety, pub. 2025-04-29; PMC12232517; also NCBI Bookshelf NBK613742, AHRQ version Jan 2025, searches Jan 2013–mid-2024). The Discussion contains exactly: "Evidence about SBAR is predominantly nursing, and evidence about I-PASS is exclusively about physicians," immediately followed by "Whether or not one generalises to the other is not established" — which directly supports the claim's inference that the moderate-certainty I-PASS data is undemonstrated for nurse-to-nurse report. Supporting details verified: SBAR studies — "Nurses were the clinicians in all included studies"; I-PASS — "We judged the certainty of evidence that the use of I-PASS reduces medical errors and adverse events as moderate," vs low for SBAR. Only caveat found: the results section says "almost all" I-PASS studies were physician-to-physician (one Khan/Kuzma study used NP/PAs in the provider role), while the discussion says "exclusively" — an internal tension in the review's own wording that the claim faithfully mirrors, and NP/PA-to-NP/PA handoff still is not nurse-to-nurse shift report, so the conclusion stands. Counter-evidence search found nursing I-PASS adaptations (PSNet primer: I-PASS "has also been shown to improve the quality of nursing handoffs"; I-PASS Institute case studies; QI/DNP projects) but these measure handoff-quality/process outcomes, not the graded medical-error/adverse-event outcomes — they do not contradict the review's characterization of its effectiveness evidence base. Source is current (most recent major synthesis as of June 2026) and high quality (AHRQ + peer-reviewed BMJ Q&S).

Claim verified against three independent indexes of the primary source. PubMed (PMID 40084437) confirms: Abt M, Schneider P, Martin L, Delmas P, Ortoleva Bucher C, "I-PASS-Structured Bedside Nursing Handovers: A Type-1 Effectiveness-Implementation Hybrid Pilot Study," Journal of Advanced Nursing (Wiley), online ~March 2025, print Vol 82(1):882-896, Jan 2026. The abstract states verbatim that bedside nursing handovers were introduced into "one surgery and one medicine ward" (Swiss setting — La Source/HES-SO Lausanne authors, wards in French-speaking Switzerland, Aug-Nov 2022) with "the 831 handovers evaluated showing significant improvements in handover quality compared to before implementation, although handover duration increased." Design is a quantitative interrupted time series within a hybrid pilot, so "significant" carries its statistical meaning; "pilot" is the article's own framing. The I-PASS used is a nursing adaptation (Identification-Patient-Action-Situation-Synthesis), matching the claim's wording and the research question's scope. Semantic Scholar and Europe PMC return the identical abstract. No retraction/correction/erratum found; peer-reviewed primary source, current (2025/2026), not marketing material — the claim even preserves the negative duration finding. Sole caveat: full-text p-values could not be inspected (Wiley returned HTTP 402 on /doi, /doi/full, and /pdfdirect despite CC-BY status), but the claim asserts nothing beyond the abstract's results statement.

Verified against PubMed PMID 40084437 (Abt M, Schneider P, Martin L, Delmas P, Ortoleva Bucher C. "I-PASS-Structured Bedside Nursing Handovers: A Type-1 Effectiveness-Implementation Hybrid Pilot Study." J Adv Nurs, Epub 2025-03-14, Jan 2026 issue; DOI 10.1111/jan.16896 — the cited URL). (1) The supporting quote is verbatim in the abstract: "...preliminary effectiveness of I-PASS-structured (Identification-Patient-Action-Situation-Synthesis) bedside nursing handovers on the handover global quality and the patients trust in nurses." (2) "End-of-shift" is explicitly supported: abstract background states "Oral end-of-shift nursing handovers can become moments of patient vulnerability." (3) Outcomes match: handover global quality and patient trust in nurses (831 handovers, surgery and medicine wards, interrupted time series, Aug-Nov 2022). (4) The parenthetical about the original physician I-PASS is correct per the I-PASS Institute mnemonic sheet and NEJM 2014 (Starmer et al.): I = Illness severity, P = Patient summary, A = Action list, S = Situation awareness/contingency planning, S = Synthesis by receiver — so the study's "Identification/Patient" labels do depart lexically from the original. Minor caveat (not refuting): the study's "Identification of the patient's condition, stable or unstable" is semantically close to the original "Illness severity," so the departure is largely in labeling/operationalization for the nursing bedside context; the claim asserts only the expansion wording differs, which is true. Source is peer-reviewed, PubMed-indexed, current (2025/2026), and matches the descriptive strength of the claim. No contradicting evidence found in web search.

Verified against the primary source (Abt et al., "I-PASS-Structured Bedside Nursing Handovers: A Type-1 Effectiveness-Implementation Hybrid Pilot Study," Journal of Advanced Nursing 2026;82:882-896, DOI 10.1111/jan.16896, PMID 40084437, ISRCTN 81701569; CC-BY full text retrieved from https://arodes.hes-so.ch/record/15752/files/AbtEtal_2025_I_PASS_Structured.pdf). The supporting quote is verbatim from the abstract. Every added detail in the claim checks out in the full text: (1) Swiss two-ward pilot — Methods: "one surgery ward and one general medicine ward in two private clinics in French-speaking Switzerland belonging to the same healthcare group"; all authors at La Source School of Nursing, HES-SO, Lausanne. (2) Statistical significance — Results: "A total of 831 handovers were observed during and evaluated"; RTHQ quality scores fitted with ARIMA(0,0,0) plus an intervention regressor whose value "was positive and significant... bedside nursing handovers in both clinics resulted in better handover quality"; Discussion: "improved handover quality significantly." (3) Duration — abstract: "handover duration increased"; full text: significant ~48-second increase in Clinic B, non-significant/unstable in Clinic A — the claim does not assert significance for duration, so no overreach. No correction, erratum, or retraction found; the paper is cited positively in 2025-2026 literature. Peer-reviewed primary source, published online 14 March 2025 (issue Feb 2026) — current for the 2024-2026 research window, not a press release or marketing material, and the claim correctly preserves the "pilot" framing.

Quote verified verbatim by downloading and grepping the raw text of the peer-reviewed publication of this exact AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV review (BMJ Quality & Safety 2025, PMC12232517): the limitations section reads "Evidence about SBAR is predominantly nursing, and evidence about I-PASS is exclusively about physicians. Whether or not one generalises to the other is not established." A second discussion sentence independently corroborates: "The evidence supporting the effect of I-PASS use on clinical outcomes is exclusively about physician handoffs and comes almost entirely from the developers." The moderate-certainty framing is accurate: both renditions state 10 studies/9 implementations provided moderate-certainty evidence that I-PASS reduces medical errors and adverse events (uprated from low), vs low-certainty for SBAR; SBAR studies all involved nurses ("Nurses were the clinicians in all included studies"). The claim's inference (moderate-certainty I-PASS outcome data not demonstrated in nurse-to-nurse report) is exactly the review's own "whether one generalises to the other is not established" statement. Caveats found: (1) the cited AHRQ PDF is behind an AWS WAF challenge and could not be fetched; the NCBI Bookshelf rendition of the AHRQ report (NBK613742, Jan 2025) phrases it as "almost all studies assessed physician-to-physician handoffs" without the verbatim "exclusively" sentence — the verbatim wording is in the peer-reviewed journal rendition of the same review (same authors/project); a citation-rendition nuance, not a substantive discrepancy. (2) Nursing I-PASS adaptations exist (Blazin 2020 Pediatr Qual Saf; AHRQ PSNet primer notes improved nursing handoff quality) but measured process/quality outcomes, not the moderate-certainty patient-safety clinical outcomes at issue, so they do not contradict the scoped claim. Source is a high-quality, current (2025) government evidence synthesis, not marketing.

Verified against PubMed PMID 40084437 (Abt M et al., 'I-PASS-Structured Bedside Nursing Handovers: A Type-1 Effectiveness-Implementation Hybrid Pilot Study', Journal of Advanced Nursing, epub 14 Mar 2025, issue Jan 2026; DOI 10.1111/jan.16896). The PubMed abstract reproduces the supporting quote verbatim: the aim was to evaluate 'feasibility, acceptability and preliminary effectiveness of I-PASS-structured (Identification-Patient-Action-Situation-Synthesis) bedside nursing handovers on the handover global quality and the patients trust in nurses' — confirming both the variant expansion and the two claimed outcomes. The 'end-of-shift' framing is supported by the abstract background ('Oral end-of-shift nursing handovers can become moments of patient vulnerability'), and the study is nursing-specific (831 nurse handovers on surgery/medicine wards). The parenthetical about the original I-PASS is accurate: per Starmer et al. (NEJM 2014) and AHRQ PSNet, the original expands as Illness severity, Patient summary, Action list, Situation awareness/contingency planning, Synthesis by receiver — so this study's Identification/Patient expansion is genuinely a departure. No contradicting source found; source is a current (2025/2026) peer-reviewed primary study, adequate for this descriptive claim. Minor caveat: Wiley full text is paywalled (HTTP 402), so verification rests on the verbatim abstract; the abstract states the variant expansion but does not itself label it an 'adaptation' — that gloss is the claim-author's accurate characterization, not the authors' explicit statement.

The quote is genuine and faithfully paraphrased: the source (Aprilia et al., "Comparison of the Effectiveness of SBAR and I-PASS Communication Methods in Nurse Handover: Literature Review," Indonesian Journal of Global Health Research, article 6437) is a literature review of only 10 studies (PubMed/Google Scholar, 2013-2023) and its abstract does conclude I-PASS is "more recommended than SBAR." However, the claim fails on two refutation criteria. (1) Source quality is insufficient for a comparative-effectiveness recommendation: IJGHR is a SINTA-3 accredited Indonesian journal (mid-low national tier, SINTA impact score 0.45, not Scopus/MEDLINE indexed), and the review is a small narrative synthesis, not a rigorous head-to-head evaluation. (2) It is contradicted by the higher-quality AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV systematic review of structured handoff protocols (PMC12232517), which explicitly recommends "a structured protocol, either I-PASS or SBAR" rather than ranking them, and states the I-PASS clinical-outcome evidence (incl. the Starmer 2014 NEJM 23% error-reduction study) is "exclusively about physician handoffs" while SBAR evidence "comes predominantly from handoffs where nurses are involved," noting "whether or not one generalises to the other is not established." Thus the IJGHR review's core ground — that I-PASS reduces medical-error risk better than SBAR specifically in NURSING handover — extrapolates physician-handoff evidence to nurses, an extrapolation the best available systematic evidence explicitly declines to make. The published I-PASS nursing evidence (I-PASS Nursing Handoff Bundle, AHRQ PSNet) shows improved verbal handoff process/communication quality, not nursing medical-error reduction. The claim should not be used to recommend I-PASS over SBAR for a nursing handoff training platform; the defensible statement is that both structured tools outperform unstructured report, with I-PASS error-reduction evidence physician-derived (moderate certainty) and SBAR nurse-derived (low certainty).

Verified verbatim against the Europe PMC abstract record for PMID 40084437 (Abt et al., J Adv Nurs 2026;82(1):882-896, DOI 10.1111/jan.16896): the aim sentence reads exactly "to evaluate the feasibility, acceptability and preliminary effectiveness of I-PASS-structured (Identification-Patient-Action-Situation-Synthesis) bedside nursing handovers on the handover global quality and the patients trust in nurses" — confirming both the adapted expansion and the two outcomes. The Background confirms end-of-shift scope: "Oral end-of-shift nursing handovers can become moments of patient vulnerability." The parenthetical about the original physician I-PASS is accurate: Starmer et al. (Pediatrics 2012, PMID 22232313; I-PASS Institute mnemonic PDF) define I = Illness severity, P = Patient summary, A = Action list, S = Situation awareness/contingency planning, S = Synthesis by receiver, developed for resident physician handoffs. The study is a peer-reviewed, prospectively registered (ISRCTN81701569) Type-1 hybrid pilot (831 handovers, two wards, Aug-Nov 2022) — primary source quality matches the claim's strength, and the claim's hedged framing ("a nursing-specific adaptation," indefinite) matches what the study itself states.

The quote is genuine (verified via the article's abstract: Khotijah & Mulyono, "Comparison of the Effectiveness of Sbar and I-PASS Communication Methods in Nurse Handover: Literature Review," Indonesian Journal of Global Health Research 7(5):175-180, DOI 10.37287/ijghr.v7i5.6437), but the claim fails on source quality and contradiction. (1) Source quality insufficient for a strong comparative-effectiveness recommendation: it is a 10-article narrative literature review (from 1,008 screened, PubMed + Google Scholar, 2013-2023) in a SINTA-3 Indonesian journal that is not Scopus/Web of Science indexed (publisher: Global Health Science Group). (2) Directly contradicted by a methodologically superior 2025 systematic review — McCarthy S, Motala A, Lawson E, Shekelle PG, "Use of structured handoff protocols for within-hospital unit transitions: a systematic review from Making Healthcare Safer IV," BMJ Qual Saf 2025;34(10):e018385 — which states: "Evidence about SBAR is predominantly nursing, and evidence about I-PASS is exclusively about physicians. Whether or not one generalises to the other is not established," and "The evidence supporting the effect of I-PASS use on clinical outcomes is exclusively about physician handoffs and comes almost entirely from the developers." The headline I-PASS error reductions (Starmer et al. 2014 NEJM) are from resident-physician signout, not nursing shift report, so no credible head-to-head nursing evidence supports recommending I-PASS over SBAR for NURSING handover. The IJGHR conclusion is an extrapolation that the AHRQ review explicitly declines to make; it should not anchor the platform's framework recommendation.

The quote is real — the IJGHR narrative review (Vol 7(5):175-180) does conclude verbatim that I-PASS is "more recommended than SBAR in nursing handover practice." However, the claim fails checklist items 2 and 3. (a) Source quality insufficient for claim strength: IJGHR is a Sinta 3 (mid-low tier) Indonesian journal from Global Health Science Group with a near-zero citation footprint; the article is a narrative literature review of only 10 articles from PubMed/Google Scholar (2013-2023), with no head-to-head nursing trials behind its comparative-effectiveness recommendation. (b) Contradicted by stronger evidence: the AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV systematic review (2025, PMC12232517) explicitly states "The evidence supporting the effect of I-PASS use on clinical outcomes is exclusively about physician handoffs" — I-PASS gets moderate-certainty evidence for error reduction ONLY in physician/resident handoffs, while SBAR is the tool whose evidence comes "predominantly from handoffs where nurses are involved" (low certainty). MHS IV makes no I-PASS-over-SBAR recommendation for nursing handover and notes the cross-professional comparison cannot be made. The IJGHR conclusion extrapolates physician-handoff error-reduction data (e.g., Starmer NEJM 2014) to nursing shift report, a claim the rigorous literature does not support. For the research question (nursing-specific shift handoff), this claim should not stand as evidence that I-PASS reduces medical errors better than SBAR in nursing.

The quote is real (abstract of Khotijah & Mulyono 2025, Indonesian Journal of Global Health Research 7(5):175-180, a 10-article narrative literature review), but the claim fails on source quality and is heavily contradicted by a superior source. The AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV systematic review (BMJ Quality & Safety, 2025, PMC12232517) found: (1) NO study has directly compared I-PASS vs SBAR head-to-head, so the quantified comparison ('only slightly superior', 'marginal not dramatic') has no direct evidential basis; (2) evidence certainty is asymmetric — MODERATE that I-PASS reduces medical errors/adverse events vs LOW for SBAR (mixed results, some studies showing no benefit) — contradicting the 'both proven effective' equivalence framing; (3) I-PASS's measured effects were substantial (23-46% reductions in medical errors, 30% in preventable adverse events), but came almost exclusively from physician-to-physician handoffs in teaching hospitals, while SBAR evidence is predominantly nursing — so asserting I-PASS effectiveness 'in nurse handover' specifically, and sizing its advantage over SBAR there, is an unsupported cross-population extrapolation. The source is a 6-page review in a low-tier Indonesian journal (site repeatedly returns 'DB Error: MySQL server has gone away'), inadequate for a comparative-effectiveness claim of this strength.

The claim fails on three checklist points. (1) Overreach of the quote: the IJGHR abstract's quoted sentence exists, but the claim's "i.e., marginal, not dramatic" gloss contradicts the SAME review's own conclusion — "The I-PASS communication method is more recommended than SBAR in nursing handover practice because it has higher effectiveness in reducing the risk of medical errors and improving the quality of information transfer." The source recommends I-PASS over SBAR; the claim spins one adverb ("slightly") into a 'differences are negligible' framing the source does not draw. (2) Contradicted by a higher-quality synthesis: the AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV systematic review (PMC12232517) rates certainty that I-PASS reduces medical errors/adverse events as MODERATE but SBAR's certainty as LOW ("no benefit was observed in a third of the [SBAR] studies"), concludes "I-PASS is the tool that has the strongest certainty of evidence in reducing medical errors," and contains NO head-to-head I-PASS-vs-SBAR comparison — so 'only slightly superior' implies a quantified comparison that does not exist; where I-PASS was measured, effects were substantial (errors 24.5→18.8/100 admissions ~23% reduction; preventable adverse events 4.7→3.3/100 ~30%), while 'both were found effective' overstates SBAR's low-certainty, inconsistent error-reduction evidence. (3) Source quality insufficient for a comparative patient-safety effectiveness claim: a 10-article literature review (PubMed/Google Scholar, 2013–2023) in the Indonesian Journal of Global Health Research — not Scopus-indexed, near-zero citations (SciSpace), publisher site returning raw "DB Error: MySQL server has gone away" — using overclaiming language ("proven to be effective"). Safe takeaway for the platform: I-PASS has the strongest (moderate-certainty) error-reduction evidence; SBAR's is low-certainty; no head-to-head sizes the gap as "slight."

The quote is authentic (confirmed in the article abstract: 10-article narrative literature review, PubMed + Google Scholar, 2013-2023, Indonesian Journal of Global Health Research), but the claim fails on three grounds. (1) Source quality insufficient for a comparative-effectiveness safety claim: IJGHR is a SINTA 3 (Indonesian mid-low tier) journal, not MEDLINE/Scopus-indexed; the article page currently returns 'DB Error: MySQL server has gone away'; a 10-study narrative review using Google Scholar as a database cannot ground a quantified 'marginal advantage' conclusion. (2) Contradicted by a higher-quality source: the 2025 AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV systematic review (PMC12232517) rates I-PASS evidence for reducing medical errors/adverse events as MODERATE certainty but SBAR evidence on the same outcomes as LOW certainty, and explicitly finds NO head-to-head SBAR vs I-PASS comparison exists — so the gap cannot be characterized as 'slight' or 'marginal' by anyone; additionally, I-PASS error-reduction evidence comes from physician-to-physician handoffs (Starmer NEJM 2014: 23% relative reduction in medical errors, 30% in preventable adverse events among residents), not nurse handover, undercutting the claim's 'in nurse handover' framing for both tools. (3) Internal inconsistency: the source's own conclusion says I-PASS 'is more recommended than SBAR... because it has higher effectiveness in reducing the risk of medical errors,' which conflicts with the claim's 'marginal, not dramatic' gloss. The defensible statement is 'I-PASS has stronger-certainty evidence; no direct comparison quantifies the difference,' not 'both proven effective with I-PASS only slightly superior.'

Verified against the full-text PDF (downloaded from https://www.journalmpci.com/index.php/jhnr/article/download/1073/512; saved at /tmp/jhnr_1073.pdf). Every specific detail in the claim matches the source verbatim. Page 6: "These studies were conducted in the United States (n=6), Canada (n=1), Iran (n=1), and Switzerland (n=1). Five studies utilized quasi-experimental designs; one used an observational design; two applied the Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) methodology; one used a prospective pre-post intervention design; and one employed a mixed-methods approach. The number of participants varied across studies, ranging from 15 to 3,177 participants (nurses and other healthcare professionals) in various care settings, including inpatient units, emergency departments, psychiatric emergency, Intensive Care Unit (ICU), Critical Care Unit (CCU), Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU), and surgery ward." Abstract confirms 9 included studies (2015-2025) and "five themes": "reduction in communication errors, improvement information quality, medical staff satisfaction, time efficiency, as well as patient and family engagement." Publication confirmed: Journal of Health and Nutrition Research, Vol. 5, No. 1, 2026, pp. 450-463, DOI 10.56303/jhnresearch.v5i1.1073, published April 1, 2026 (current, not outdated); authors from Faculty of Nursing, Universitas Padjadjaran; PRISMA-ScR scoping review across ScienceDirect/EBSCO/PubMed/Scopus with dual-reviewer extraction — peer-reviewed academic work, not marketing. The claim reuses the article's own hedged "was associated with" language and transparently discloses the weak designs, so claim strength matches source strength. No contradicting evidence found; independent literature corroborates I-PASS handoff benefits (Starmer et al., NEJM 2014; AHRQ PSNet I-PASS reviews). Caveats that do not refute the claim (article-internal flaws, accurately transmitted by the claim): design counts sum to 10 across 9 studies; Table 1 lists the largest sample as 3,711 nurses while the narrative text says 3,177 (claim's "~3,177" mirrors the narrative); journal is SINTA 2 (mid-tier Indonesian accreditation), so the review should be weighted as corroborating rather than definitive evidence.

Verified against the full-text PDF downloaded from the journal (https://www.journalmpci.com/index.php/jhnr/article/download/1073/512; Penu, Mediawati & Agustina, "Impact of the I-PASS Communication Method on Nurse Handover Quality in Hospital Settings: A Scoping Review," Journal of Health and Nutrition Research 5(1):450-463, published 1 April 2026, DOI 10.56303/jhnresearch.v5i1.1073). Every element of the claim matches the source. Page 455 verbatim: "These studies were conducted in the United States (n=6), Canada (n=1), Iran (n=1), and Switzerland (n=1). Five studies utilized quasi-experimental designs; one used an observational design; two applied the Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) methodology; one used a prospective pre-post intervention design; and one employed a mixed-methods approach. The number of participants varied across studies, ranging from 15 to 3,177 participants (nurses and other healthcare professionals) in various care settings, including inpatient units, emergency departments, psychiatric emergency, Intensive Care Unit (ICU), Critical Care Unit (CCU), Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU), and surgery ward." The abstract and Results name exactly the five claimed themes ("reduction in communication errors, improvement information quality, medical staff satisfaction, time efficiency, as well as patient and family engagement"), and the review is nurse-handover-specific (PCC population "All registered nurses"; 9 included studies 2015-2025; PRISMA-ScR with dual independent extraction). The claim's hedged "was associated with" mirrors the source's own wording — no overreach. WebSearch found no contradicting evidence; independent literature (Starmer 2017 I-PASS Nursing Handoff Bundle, PMID 28679836; Abt 2026 J Adv Nurs hybrid pilot, DOI 10.1111/jan.16896; AHRQ Making Healthcare Safer IV handoff review) is directionally consistent. Caveats that do NOT rise to refutation: (1) internal inconsistency in the article — Table 1 lists the largest study (Aiss et al. 2025) as "3711 Nurses" while the narrative says 3,177; the claim's "~3,177" reflects the review's narrative text; (2) the venue is a low-profile Indonesian journal (Media Publikasi Cendekia Indonesia, e-ISSN 2829-9760, claims SINTA 2) whose scope is nutrition-adjacent, and a scoping review does not grade evidence quality — so the claim should be used as "what this review reported," not as strong causal proof of I-PASS effectiveness; the claim as written is calibrated accordingly. Current (search window Dec 2024-Sep 2025), peer-reviewed, not marketing.

Fetched the cited article page and its full-text PDF (https://www.journalmpci.com/index.php/jhnr/article/download/1073/512; DOI 10.56303/jhnresearch.v5i1.1073, Journal of Health and Nutrition Research, published April 2026: "Impact of the I-PASS Communication Method on Nurse Handover Quality in Hospital Settings: A Scoping Review" by Penu, Mediawati, Agustina). Every granular detail of the claim is confirmed verbatim in the full text: "A total of 9 studies were included in this review" (2015-2025 inclusion window); "These studies were conducted in the United States (n=6), Canada (n=1), Iran (n=1), and Switzerland (n=1)"; "Five studies utilized quasi-experimental designs; one used an observational design; two applied the Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) methodology; one used a prospective pre-post intervention design; and one employed a mixed-methods approach"; "ranging from 15 to 3,177 participants (nurses and other healthcare professionals)"; settings listed as "inpatient units, emergency departments, psychiatric emergency, Intensive Care Unit (ICU), Critical Care Unit (CCU), Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU), and surgery ward"; and the five impact themes (reduced communication errors, improved information quality, medical staff satisfaction, time efficiency, patient/family engagement) appear verbatim in both abstract and results. The review is explicitly about NURSE handovers, and the claim preserves the source's hedged "was associated with" wording without causal overreach. Web searches found no contradicting evidence; the broader literature (NEJM 2014 I-PASS trial, AHRQ PSNet "I-PASS handover system: a decade of evidence demands action," and a PSNet-indexed I-PASS nursing handoff bundle study) is directionally consonant. Claim is current (April 2026). Caveat on weight, not accuracy: the journal is a modest Indonesian outlet (MPCI publisher, SINTA 2 national accreditation; its Scopus indexing appears to still be under CSAB review, and a nursing review in a "health and nutrition" journal is a scope mismatch), and the review itself notes implementation challenges (environmental interruptions, need for managerial support) plus US/Europe-dominant evidence — so it should be treated as supporting rather than anchor evidence, ideally alongside the NEJM/AHRQ I-PASS literature. But the claim accurately and conservatively describes what this primary source reports, so it is not refuted.

Verified against the full PDF of the source (downloaded from journalmpci.com/index.php/jhnr/article/download/1073/512: Penu, Mediawati & Agustina, "Impact of the I-PASS Communication Method on Nurse Handover Quality in Hospital Settings: A Scoping Review," J Health Nutr Research 5(1), April 2026). Every figure in the claim appears verbatim: (1) narrative p.6: "A multicentre study conducted at nine pediatric hospitals by Starmer, (2017) reported a 23% reduction in medical errors and a 30% reduction in preventable adverse events"; (2) Table 1 row Cardona et al. 2021 (NICU, neonatal nurse practitioners): "Average avoidable interruptions per handover decreased from 4 to 0.3 (92% reduction). The rate of handovers with zero interruptions increased from 11% to 80%"; (3) Table 1 row Starmer et al. 2017 (USA, 90 Nurses, prospective pre-post, Paediatric ICU): "illness severity assessment increased from 37% to 67%, and to-do list increased from 35% to 100%... No significant change in median handover duration (18.8 min vs. 19.9 min)"; (4) "A zero high harm event rate was maintained" (Ais Aiss et al. 2025 row; narrative ties it to "an oncology hospital", ref to BMJ Open Quality 2025;14(3) tertiary oncology hospital). The claim's caution is also confirmed: the table's Blazin et al. 2020 row (180 nurses, inpatient/imaging/procedural units) lists results IDENTICAL to the Starmer 2017 row ("Illness severity assessment increased from 37% to 67% and To Do List increased from 35% to 100%... interruptions decreased from 170 to 78 per 100 handovers... 18.8 min vs. 19,9 min"). Independent cross-checks show the duplicated figures genuinely belong to Starmer et al., BMJ Qual Saf 2017 (PubMed 28679836 abstract: illness severity 37% vs 67% p=0.001; to-do list 35% vs 100% p<0.001; interruptions 67% vs 40%; duration 18.8 vs 19.9 min p=0.48), while Blazin 2020 (Pediatr Qual Saf, St. Jude, PMID 32766496) actually reported process-adoption outcomes — so "transcription error" is a sound inference. Only qualification: the 23%/30% nine-hospital figures truly originate from Starmer et al. NEJM 2014 (10.1056/NEJMsa1405556: errors 24.5→18.8/100 admissions = 23%; preventable AEs 4.7→3.3/100 = 30%), which the review misdates as 2017 — but the claim's careful "attributed to Starmer 2017" wording reports the review's attribution without endorsing it. Claim is a faithful, appropriately caveated description of a current (2026) source; not refuted.

Verified against the full PDF of the review (Penu, Mediawati & Agustina, "Impact of the I-PASS Communication Method on Nurse Handover Quality in Hospital Settings: A Scoping Review," J Health & Nutrition Research 5(1), 2026, doi:10.56303/jhnresearch.v5i1.1073; PDF galley journalmpci.com/index.php/jhnr/article/download/1073/512). Every figure in the claim is present: (1) Theme 1 narrative states verbatim "A multicentre study conducted at nine pediatric hospitals by Starmer, (2017) reported a 23% reduction in medical errors and a 30% reduction in preventable adverse events"; (2) Table 1 row (Cardona et al., 2021, NICU, neonatal nurse practitioners): "Average avoidable interruptions per handover decreased from 4 to 0.3 (92% reduction). The rate of handovers with zero interruptions increased from 11% to 80%"; (3) Table 1 row (Starmer et al., 2017, USA, 90 Nurses, prospective pre-post, Paediatric ICU): "illness severity assessment increased from 37% to 67%, and to-do list increased from 35% to 100%"; (4) narrative + ref 24 (Ait Aiss et al., BMJ Open Quality 2025, tertiary oncology hospital): "helped maintain zero high-harm events" / table: "A zero high harm event rate was maintained"; (5) "No significant change in median handover duration (18.8 min vs. 19.9 min)" appears in Table 1 and the Discussion. The caution is also confirmed: the Blazin et al. 2020 table row repeats the Starmer 2017 results verbatim (37%→67%, 35%→100%, interruptions 67%→40% and 170→78 per 100 handovers, "18.8 min vs. 19,9 min"). Cross-check with PubMed PMID 28679836 (Starmer et al., BMJ Qual Saf 2017;26:949-57) confirms those exact figures (37%→67% p=0.001, 35%→100% p<0.001, 18.8 vs 19.9 min p=0.48, pediatric ICU) belong to Starmer 2017, so the duplication under Blazin 2020 is a genuine transcription error in the review — the claim's caution is accurate. The claim is purely descriptive of what the review reports, hedges correctly ("attributed to Starmer 2017" — the 23%/30% actually derive from Starmer et al. NEJM 2014, a miscitation the claim's wording accommodates), and itself flags the review's reliability problem. Minor unflagged sloppiness exists in the review (e.g., its numbered ref 23 for the NICU study points to an unrelated 2016 vital-signs paper, and the Discussion re-attributes the same 23%/30% to Ransom & Winters 2018/Soares 2023), but this does not contradict the claim — it reinforces the claim's transcription-error warning. Not outdated (review 2026, primaries 2014-2025); not marketing material.

Verified against the full-text PDF downloaded from the journal (https://www.journalmpci.com/index.php/jhnr/article/download/1073/512; local copy /tmp/jhnr1073.pdf; Penu, Mediawati & Agustina, JHNR 5(1) 2026, DOI 10.56303/jhnresearch.v5i1.1073). Every figure in the claim appears verbatim: (1) Theme 1: "A multicentre study conducted at nine pediatric hospitals by Starmer, (2017) reported a 23% reduction in medical errors and a 30% reduction in preventable adverse events"; (2) Table 1 Cardona et al. 2021 NICU row: "Average avoidable interruptions per handover decreased from 4 to 0.3 (92% reduction). The rate of handovers with zero interruptions increased from 11% to 80%"; (3) Table 1 Starmer et al. 2017 row: "90 Nurses... Prospective pre-post intervention... Paediatric ICU... illness severity assessment increased from 37% to 67%, and to-do list increased from 35% to 100%"; (4) Ais Aiss et al. 2025 row "A zero high harm event rate was maintained" plus the oncology-hospital narrative sentence (ref 24, BMJ Open Quality 2025 tertiary oncology hospital); (5) "No significant change in median handover duration (18.8 min vs. 19.9 min)" in Table 1 and Theme 4. The caution is also accurate: the Blazin et al. 2020 row carries results text identical to the Starmer 2017 row (37%→67%, 35%→100%, interruptions 67%→40%, 170→78 per 100 handovers, 18.8 vs 19.9 min). External cross-check confirms the duplication is an error in the review, exactly as the claim flags: the real Starmer 2017 BMJ Qual Saf study (PMID 28679836; PSNet) reports 67%→40% interruptions and 18.8 vs 19.9 min, while the real Blazin 2020 (Pediatr Qual Saf, PMID 32766496, St. Jude) reports adherence/perceived-error outcomes, not these figures. Residual nuance, not refutation-grade: the review's narrative "Starmer (2017)" for the nine-site 23%/30% result actually corresponds to Starmer et al. NEJM 2014 (the review's own ref 6, "Changes in Medical Errors after Implementation of a Handoff Program"), but the claim only states the review ATTRIBUTED the figures to Starmer 2017 (true) and itself warns about the review's citation reliability. Source is a low-tier Indonesian OA journal, but the claim's strength is calibrated to that: it reports the review's contents descriptively and self-flags the internal transcription error; the underlying numbers also trace to genuine primary studies. Not outdated (review 2026, primaries 2014–2025); not marketing material.

Quote is verbatim-real (verified in the article PDF) but the claim fails on sourcing and on contradiction at the citation root. (1) The sentence is introduction/background rhetoric in an I-PASS scoping review (Penu et al. 2026, J Health and Nutrition Research 5(1):1073), not a finding of the review's own synthesis — mislabeled 'primary'. (2) Citation-chain trace: the resuscitation-status/pending-results sentence cites ref 12 = Omidi 2023 (4-page Iranian pre/post pilot, 47 nurses, no control, JRDNM 20(2)), which in turn cites Thompson et al. 2011 (Postgrad Med J 87:340-4, PMID 21310805) — a study of ISBAR (an SBAR-family tool) in junior medical officers (physicians, not nurses) that found the OPPOSITE: 'audio-tape data demonstrated increased transfer of key clinical information' after ISBAR introduction; the omissions were a property of unstructured BASELINE handover, not of SBAR. The chain inverts an SBAR-variant success into an SBAR weakness. (3) The 'interruptions' leg cites ref 13 = Azmi 2025, a same-journal, same-institution (Universitas Padjadjaran) self-citation that itself calls SBAR 'widely recognized as an effective tool'; and the review's own abstract concedes I-PASS also faces 'environmental interruptions', so interruption vulnerability is not an SBAR-vs-I-PASS differential. (4) Source quality insufficient: JHNR (MPCI, Indonesia, ISSN 2829-9760) is not Scopus/MEDLINE indexed (Scopus CSAB application pending per its own site), a nutrition-titled journal publishing an out-of-scope nursing review with multiple same-journal self-citations (refs 11, 13). (5) Mainstream evidence qualifies the claim: Müller 2018 systematic review (BMJ Open 8:e022202, PMID 30139905) found moderate evidence SBAR implementation IMPROVES patient safety. Note: treating code status/pending results as high-risk omission elements remains defensible — but from Thompson 2011/I-PASS/Handoff CEX literature, not from this third-hand misattributed assertion.
